refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability of ≥0.97 (97%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the system is available for productive operation to total scheduled operational time, accounting for planned maintenance, unplanned downtime, and safety-driven shutdowns.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-004 is a key performance indicator (KPI) that directly impacts customer satisfaction and mission success. Refining it requires explicit definition of: (1) the measurement boundary (what constitutes 'available'), (2) the evaluation period (e.g., 30 days, 90 days, 1 year), (3) exclusions (planned maintenance windows, customer-initiated pauses), and (4) the role of safety-driven shutdowns. High safety criticality demands that availability not be achieved at the expense of safety; therefore, safety-driven unavailability is acceptable and must be distinguished from failure-driven unavailability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous telemetry collection of system state (operational, maintenance, shutdown, failed) with timestamped logs. Availability calculated as: (Total Scheduled Time − Unplanned Downtime − Safety Shutdowns) / Total Scheduled Time. Audit against customer-agreed evaluation period and acceptance threshold.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall detect and log all state transitions (operational, maintenance, shutdown, failure) with sub-second precision.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall minimize unplanned downtime by implementing predictive maintenance and fault tolerance mechanisms.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time availability metrics and historical availability reports to the Site Manager and Project Manager.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE solution shall distinguish between safety-driven unavailability (acceptable) and failure-driven unavailability (unacceptable) in availability calculations.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a centralized availability monitoring and reporting subsystem that continuously tracks system state, logs transitions, calculates availability metrics in real-time, and generates reports; couple with predictive maintenance algorithms and redundancy strategies to minimize unplanned downtime.

DP-B: Distribute availability monitoring across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules, with each module reporting its own uptime; aggregate at the NGHE Solution level using a weighted availability formula.

DP-C: Implement a lightweight state machine at the NGHE Solution level that tracks only high-level availability (on/off), delegating detailed fault detection and recovery to individual subsystems; report aggregate availability without sub-component granularity.

DP-D: Establish a dedicated Availability Management Service that integrates telemetry from all modules, applies machine learning to predict failures, orchestrates preventive actions, and maintains a real-time availability dashboard accessible to stakeholders.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Establish a dedicated Availability Management Service that integrates telemetry from all modules, applies machine learning to predict failures, orchestrates preventive actions, and maintains a real-time availability dashboard accessible to stakeholders.

potential_sources: DP-D requires bidirectional coupling with Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services via Telemetry interface (inbound) and SafetyAlert interface (inbound) to receive state and fault data.
DP-D couples to Site Manager and Project Manager via a reporting interface (outbound) for dashboard and KPI delivery.
DP-D couples to Safety Services to ensure safety-driven shutdowns are correctly classified and do not penalize availability metrics.

Potential coupling risk: If Availability Management Service becomes a bottleneck for real-time telemetry processing, it could introduce latency in safety-critical alerts. Mitigation: Separate safety-critical alert path from availability analytics path.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D exhibits controlled coupling through well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking for orchestration). The design separates concerns: safety-critical paths remain independent; availability analytics is secondary. This is acceptable for a KPI-focused DP.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: All modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) emit standardized telemetry and state-change events via the Telemetry interface.
Assumption: The evaluation period and availability target are agreed with the customer before system deployment.
Risk: If telemetry is incomplete or delayed, availability calculations will be inaccurate. Mitigation: Implement telemetry validation and gap-filling logic.

Risk: Machine learning models for failure prediction may have false positives, triggering unnecessary preventive actions and reducing actual availability. Mitigation: Validate ML models against historical data; use conservative thresholds.

Risk: High safety criticality means safety-driven shutdowns must be correctly identified and excluded from failure-driven unavailability. Misclassification could mask real failures. Mitigation: Implement explicit safety shutdown tagging in SafetyAlert interface.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-D is sound but should be refined to explicitly separate: (1) real-time safety-critical alert processing (must not be delayed by analytics), (2) availability analytics (can tolerate minor latency), and (3) predictive maintenance orchestration (must coordinate with Safety Services to avoid conflicts). Consider a two-tier architecture: a fast safety path and a slower analytics path.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system boundary that integrates Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. The Availability Management Service is a logical subsystem within NGHE Solution that owns the availability KPI and coordinates across all lower-level modules.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound telemetry from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules. Carries state, fault, and performance data. Used by Availability Management Service to calculate availability metrics and detect anomalies.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Inbound safety alerts from Safety Services. Carries safety-critical events and shutdown commands. Availability Management Service must tag these as 'safety-driven unavailability' and exclude from failure-driven metrics.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outbound tasking commands from Availability Management Service to Vehicle and Teleoperation modules. Used to orchestrate preventive maintenance actions and graceful shutdowns based on predictive analytics.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Outbound availability metrics and KPI reports from NGHE Solution to Site Manager and Project Manager dashboards. Includes real-time availability percentage, downtime breakdown, and predictive alerts.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural owner of the availability KPI because it is the top-level system boundary. The Availability Management Service logically resides here and has clear ownership of the KPI. Minimal diffusion: all telemetry aggregation and reporting is centralized. High containment: the service integrates data from lower modules but does not duplicate logic across them.

Vehicle module owns its own operational availability (uptime of vehicle subsystems), but it should not own the system-level availability KPI. If Availability Management Service were placed in Vehicle, it would create artificial coupling and diffusion: Teleoperation and Safety Services would need to report to Vehicle, violating architectural independence. Not recommended.

Teleoperation module owns its own operational availability (uptime of remote control and communication), but it should not own the system-level availability KPI. Placing Availability Management Service here would create similar diffusion issues as Vehicle. Not recommended.

Safety Services owns safety-critical shutdown logic and must emit SafetyAlert events. However, it should not own the system-level availability KPI because availability is a performance metric, not a safety function. Coupling Safety Services to availability analytics could compromise safety-critical responsiveness. Not recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Availability Management Service is best contained within NGHE Solution because: (1) NGHE Solution is the system-level boundary that owns the KPI, (2) it has clear visibility into all lower modules via standardized interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert), (3) it avoids artificial coupling and diffusion across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, and (4) it allows safety-critical paths to remain independent and unencumbered by analytics processing.
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution becomes a monolithic container, the Availability Management Service could grow large and difficult to maintain. Mitigation: Implement Availability Management Service as a well-defined, independently testable subsystem within NGHE Solution with clear interfaces to lower modules.

Risk: Telemetry interface may become a bottleneck if all modules emit high-frequency data. Mitigation: Implement telemetry filtering and aggregation at the module level; only send summary data to Availability Management Service.

Risk: If predictive maintenance orchestration (via Tasking interface) conflicts with ongoing operations, it could reduce actual availability. Mitigation: Coordinate with Vehicle and Teleoperation modules to schedule preventive actions during low-utilization windows.

Risk: Misclassification of safety-driven shutdowns vs. failures could mask real issues. Mitigation: Implement explicit safety shutdown tagging in SafetyAlert interface and validate classification logic with Safety Manager.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Assign Availability Management Service to NGHE Solution with the following refinements: (1) Define a clear subsystem boundary and interface contract for Availability Management Service, (2) Implement telemetry filtering at the module level to reduce data volume, (3) Establish a coordination protocol with Safety Services to ensure safety shutdowns are correctly tagged and excluded from failure metrics, (4) Create a separate fast-path for safety-critical alerts that bypasses analytics processing, (5) Document the availability calculation formula and evaluation period in the system specification.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (Availability Management Service) directly addresses FR-NGHE-004 by implementing continuous monitoring, real-time metric calculation, and reporting. The DP includes predictive maintenance and fault tolerance mechanisms to minimize unplanned downtime, which is essential to achieving the ≥0.97 availability target. The DP is traceable to the FR and does not introduce unnecessary complexity.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk 1: If Availability Management Service logic is scattered across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules instead of centralized in NGHE Solution, availability calculations will be inconsistent and difficult to audit. Mitigation: Enforce centralized ownership in NGHE Solution.

Diffusion Risk 2: If telemetry collection is not standardized across modules, Availability Management Service will struggle to aggregate data. Mitigation: Define a standard Telemetry interface schema and enforce compliance.

Diffusion Risk 3: If safety-driven shutdowns are not explicitly tagged in SafetyAlert events, Availability Management Service may misclassify them as failures. Mitigation: Implement explicit shutdown classification in Safety Services.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate IM boundary for Availability Management Service. It provides clear ownership, avoids artificial coupling across lower modules, and allows safety-critical paths to remain independent. The service integrates data from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services via well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking). Containment score of 0.95 for NGHE Solution is justified.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-004 is feasible through continuous telemetry collection and automated availability calculation. The Availability Management Service will maintain timestamped logs of all state transitions and generate availability reports at the end of the evaluation period. Verification requires: (1) agreement on evaluation period and availability target with customer, (2) definition of what constitutes 'available' vs. 'unavailable', (3) clear tagging of safety-driven shutdowns, and (4) audit of telemetry data for completeness and accuracy.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The allocation of FR-NGHE-004 to NGHE Solution is clear. The sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-004.1 through FR-NGHE-004.4) are allocated to specific functions: state detection (Availability Management Service), downtime minimization (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services with coordination from Availability Management Service), metrics reporting (Availability Management Service), and safety classification (Safety Services with validation by Availability Management Service). Each sub-FR has a clear owner and interface.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Refine the Telemetry interface specification to define the schema for state-change events, fault reports, and performance metrics. Ensure all modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) emit standardized telemetry.

Revision 2: Refine the SafetyAlert interface specification to include an explicit 'shutdown_type' field that distinguishes between safety-driven shutdowns and failure-driven shutdowns. This is critical for correct availability calculation.

Revision 3: Define the availability calculation formula in the system specification, including: (a) what time periods are excluded (e.g., planned maintenance), (b) how safety-driven shutdowns are handled, (c) the evaluation period, and (d) the acceptance threshold (≥0.97).

Revision 4: Establish a coordination protocol between Availability Management Service and Safety Services to ensure that predictive maintenance actions do not conflict with safety-critical operations.

Revision 5: Implement a two-tier telemetry architecture: (a) fast path for safety-critical alerts (bypasses analytics), (b) slower path for availability analytics (can tolerate minor latency). This ensures safety is not compromised by analytics processing.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-004 is a system-level KPI that requires decomposition into sub-FRs to be implementable. The refined FR and selected DP are sound, and the IM assignment to NGHE Solution is appropriate. However, the sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-004.1 through FR-NGHE-004.4) require further decomposition to define the detailed design of the Availability Management Service, including: (1) state machine design, (2) telemetry aggregation logic, (3) availability calculation algorithm, (4) predictive maintenance algorithms, (5) reporting and dashboard design, and (6) safety shutdown classification logic. Proceeding to the next decomposition level will clarify ownership, interfaces, and implementation details.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall detect and log all state transitions (operational, maintenance, shutdown, failure) with sub-second precision.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall minimize unplanned downtime by implementing predictive maintenance and fault tolerance mechanisms.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time availability metrics and historical availability reports to the Site Manager and Project Manager.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE solution shall distinguish between safety-driven unavailability (acceptable) and failure-driven unavailability (unacceptable) in availability calculations.

assumptions: Assumption 1: All modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) emit standardized telemetry via the Telemetry interface.
Assumption 2: The customer will agree on an evaluation period (e.g., 30 days, 90 days, 1 year) and confirm the availability target (≥0.97) before system deployment.
Assumption 3: Safety-driven shutdowns are explicitly tagged in SafetyAlert events and can be distinguished from failure-driven shutdowns.
Assumption 4: Predictive maintenance algorithms can be validated against historical data and will not introduce excessive false positives.
Assumption 5: The Availability Management Service can be implemented as a subsystem within NGHE Solution without creating a bottleneck or single point of failure.
open_risks: Risk 1: Telemetry completeness and accuracy. If modules do not emit complete or timely telemetry, availability calculations will be inaccurate. Mitigation: Implement telemetry validation and gap-filling logic; audit telemetry data regularly.

Risk 2: Safety-driven shutdown misclassification. If safety shutdowns are not correctly tagged, they may be counted as failures, masking real issues. Mitigation: Implement explicit shutdown classification in Safety Services; validate with Safety Manager.

Risk 3: Predictive maintenance false positives. ML models may trigger unnecessary preventive actions, reducing actual availability. Mitigation: Validate models against historical data; use conservative thresholds; coordinate with operations team.

Risk 4: Telemetry bottleneck. High-frequency telemetry from all modules could overwhelm the Availability Management Service. Mitigation: Implement telemetry filtering and aggregation at the module level.

Risk 5: Safety-critical alert latency. If analytics processing delays safety-critical alerts, it could compromise safety. Mitigation: Implement a separate fast path for safety alerts that bypasses analytics.
evidence_needed: Evidence 1: Customer agreement on evaluation period and availability target (≥0.97 or alternative).
Evidence 2: Telemetry interface specification with schema for state-change events, fault reports, and performance metrics.
Evidence 3: SafetyAlert interface specification with explicit shutdown_type field.
Evidence 4: Availability calculation formula and algorithm, including handling of planned maintenance, safety shutdowns, and failure-driven downtime.
Evidence 5: Predictive maintenance algorithm design and validation results against historical data.
Evidence 6: Telemetry architecture design (two-tier: fast path for safety, slower path for analytics).
Evidence 7: Coordination protocol between Availability Management Service and Safety Services.
Evidence 8: Dashboard and reporting design for Site Manager and Project Manager.
Evidence 9: Test plan for availability measurement and KPI verification.
